text: The surgeon opens the peritoneal leaf of the transverse mesocolon medially, facilitating the dissection from the body and tail of the pancreas due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery. The peritoneal leaf of the left mesocolon is etched caudally until the inferior mesenteric vein is identified.

The surgeon performs vascular dissection, revealing the left colic vein, which is separated from the left colic artery. The vein is sealed with a radiofrequency device at its confluence with the anterior mesenteric vein. Subsequently, the left colic artery is sectioned.

The surgeon etches the peritoneal leaf of the mesosigma and proximal rectum from cranial to caudal. The vascular dissection begins posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery and continues along the inferior mesenteric vein, which is separated from the left colic artery running behind it.

During the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery, the hypogastric plexus is visibly preserved. The video shows the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery from the aorta and the bifurcation of the left colic artery from the main pedicle. The left colic vein is seen descending from the splenic flexure to its confluence with the inferior mesenteric veins.
The surgeon sections the inferior mesenteric vein, superior rectal artery, and sigmoid artery using the low-tie technique, without the use of clips or suture devices.
